      Oh Elon=C2=A0
   =20
 =20
Should Twitter have an edit button, letting you edit tweets after you post =
them? The answer to this question is obviously =E2=80=9Cno,=E2=80=9D[1] but=
 for some reason a lot of Twitter users vocally believe the answer is =E2=
=80=9Cyes.=E2=80=9D Here I do not want to argue about the substance, though=
. I just want to point out that if you have a view on this question, a Twit=
ter Inc. board member is currently conducting a poll about it, on Twitter. =
(=E2=80=9CYes=E2=80=9D is outrunning =E2=80=9Cno=E2=80=9D by about 3 to 1.)=
 =E2=80=9CThe consequences of this poll will be important,=E2=80=9D tweeted=
 Twitter=E2=80=99s chief executive officer. So go vote, and whichever side =
wins will happen I guess.Yesterday  I suggested that Elon Musk, Twitter=E2=
=80=99s new largest shareholder, should join the board of directors and liv=
e-tweet the board meetings. Today Twitter announced that Musk is in fact jo=
ining the board, and I feel like it=E2=80=99s going to be so much worse tha=
n live-tweeting the board meetings? It=E2=80=99s going to be:=09Musk thinks=
 of a way to make Twitter more annoying (the edit button, more =E2=80=9Cfre=
e speech,=E2=80=9D somehow more Dogecoin integration, etc.).=09Musk conduct=
s a Twitter poll asking if Twitter should do the thing.=09=E2=80=9CYes, be =
more annoying=E2=80=9D wins in a landslide; how could it not.=09Musk shows =
up at the board meeting to be like =E2=80=9Csee, our users want this annoyi=
ng thing.=E2=80=9D=09Also he owns the most shares.=09The board does the ann=
oying thing.=09Emboldened, Musk returns to Step 1.I also joked yesterday th=
at Musk would be the first Twitter user ever to join the board. That is not=
 literally true, but it does=C2=A0feel=C2=A0true.=C2=A0My basic model of ho=
w Twitter works is that everyone who works at Twitter hates the product and=
 its users, which creates=C2=A0a healthy tension. Twitter=E2=80=99s users p=
ush Twitter to be more Twitter-like, to appeal to the weirdest and most add=
icted tendencies of its most online power users. But Twitter=E2=80=99s empl=
oyees and executives push it to be less Twitter-like, because they don=E2=
=80=99t use it, and it ends up being just about Twitter-like enough. If you=
 put Twitter=E2=80=99s most addicted users in charge, it would be an unusab=
le nightmare of harassment and weirdness. Twitter=E2=80=99s very most addic=
ted user is now in charge.What else, what else. Is Musk =E2=80=9Cin charge=
=E2=80=9D of Twitter? He is just one of 12 board members.[2] He=E2=80=99s t=
he largest shareholder, but with 9.2%=C2=A0 of the stock he=E2=80=99s not r=
eally a controlling shareholder. As part of his deal to join the board, he =
signed a standstill agreement=C2=A0promising not to acquire more than 14.9%=
 of the stock, so he can=E2=80=99t threaten a hostile takeover if he doesn=
=E2=80=99t get his way on the board.=C2=A0The company is run by a CEO and e=
xecutives who were not appointed by Musk and do not report directly to him.=
On the other hand, Musk is surely Twitter=E2=80=99s noisiest board member. =
He is=C2=A0the biggest shareholder. Since Donald Trump was kicked off Twitt=
er, Musk is arguably its most important=C2=A0user. He has tweeted memes com=
paring Twitter=E2=80=99s CEO, Parag Agrawal,  to Stalin. (=E2=80=9CThis cou=
ld be setting up for another leadership change,=E2=80=9D  Cathie Wood said =
of Musk=E2=80=99s stake.) And he=E2=80=99s gonna show up at board meetings =
with those polls. It is hard not to conclude that he will have an outsized =
influence on the board, that he will not be especially deferential to Agraw=
al=E2=80=99s vision,=C2=A0and that Twitter might shift its strategy in some=
 way =E2=80=94=C2=A0possibly toward less moderation of misinformation and a=
buse =E2=80=94=C2=A0because of his presence.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CLooking forward =
to working with Parag & Twitter board to make significant improvements to T=
witter in coming months,=E2=80=9D he tweeted today.As we discussed yesterda=
y, Musk disclosed his 9.2% stake in Twitter on Schedule 13G, which is tradi=
tionally used by passive investors. You are not allowed to file a 13G =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0you have to file the more detailed and informative Schedule 13D=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0if you have =E2=80=9Cacquired the securities with any purpo=
se, or with the effect, of changing or influencing the control of the issue=
r.=E2=80=9D Did Musk acquire his Twitter stock with the purpose of influenc=
ing the control of Twitter?I don=E2=80=99t know? I think it is quite obviou=
s that if you acquire 9.2% of the stock, file a 13G, and immediately launch=
 a proxy fight to replace the CEO and board of directors, you have broken t=
he rules. It is fairly obvious that if you acquire 9.2% of the stock, file =
a 13G, and go to the company saying =E2=80=9CI own 9.2% and will launch a p=
roxy fight unless you appoint me to your board of directors,=E2=80=9D you h=
ave also broken the rules. If you acquire 9.2% of the stock, file a 13G, tw=
eet some ominous polls, cook up various schemes to tweak the product, have =
some basically polite=C2=A0chats with the CEO and get appointed to the boar=
d, have you broken the rules? I think that you have not clearly broken the =
letter of the rules.[3] Nonetheless I think that the SEC=E2=80=99s Elon Mus=
k Division is going to be annoyed. Passive investors aren=E2=80=99t usually=
 asked to sign standstills promising not to buy more stock.There is also a=
=C2=A0timing=C2=A0point that they might find annoying. I don=E2=80=99t know=
 when Elon Musk bought his 9.2% stake in Twitter, or how, or even what he a=
ctually bought. (Maybe it=E2=80=99s at-the-money physically settled call op=
tions?) If he had filed a Schedule 13D I would know, because Schedule 13D r=
equires a description of =E2=80=9Cany transactions in the class of securiti=
es reported on that were effected during the past sixty days=E2=80=9D as we=
ll as =E2=80=9Cthe amount of funds or other consideration used or to be use=
d in making the purchases=E2=80=9D; Schedule 13G does not.But Musk=E2=80=99=
s 13G does say on the cover that the =E2=80=9CDate of Event which Requires =
Filing of this Statement=E2=80=9D was March 14, 2022, and the 13G was filed=
 yesterday, April 4. The 13G rules require a filing within 10 calendar days=
 after you acquire 5% of the stock.[4] If March 14 is when Musk=C2=A0hit 5%=

At some level I  do not quite understand the geopolitical calculus around R=
ussia paying or not paying its debts? Like:=09Russia has dollars that it ho=
lds in U.S. bank accounts, which have been more or less immobilized by sanc=
tions.=09It seems like the U.S. would like it if Russia used those dollars =
to pay its foreign debts, since (1) the dollars would go to foreign investo=
rs (U.S. pension funds, etc.), (2) Russia would have fewer dollars=C2=A0and=
 (3) Russia wouldn=E2=80=99t get anything in exchange.=09It seems like Russ=
ia might not want to use those dollars to pay its foreign debts, since (1) =
then it would have fewer dollars, (2) it wouldn=E2=80=99t get anything in e=
xchange and (3) the dollars would go to creditors from =E2=80=9Cunfriendly=
=E2=80=9D countries.=09In general=C2=A0Russia would prefer not to default o=
n its debts, in order to maintain the confidence of international capital m=
arkets, but it=E2=80=99s not like Russia has much access to international c=
apital markets anyway? The=C2=A0sanctions=C2=A0are the main problem for Rus=
sia, as well as a general capital boycott due to its brutal invasion of Ukr=
aine, and paying bond interest on time won=E2=80=99t fix any of that.But in=
 fact Russia has worked hard to pay its dollar bondholders=C2=A0and has bee=
n successful so far, and now the U.S. is  trying harder to stop it:Russia=
=E2=80=99s efforts to avoid a sovereign default took another blow after the=
 U.S. Treasury halted dollar debt payments from the country=E2=80=99s accou=
nts at U.S. banks.The decision further complicates Russia=E2=80=99s attempt=
s to keep meeting debt obligations amid the sanctions imposed after it inva=
ded Ukraine. As the government tries to sidestep its first external default=
 in about a century, those restrictions have hampered and delayed the proce=
ss of transferring money to bond holders. =E2=80=A6The U.S. announcement is=
 intended to force Russia into either draining its domestic dollar reserves=
 or spending new revenue to make bond payments, or else go into default, ac=
cording to a spokesperson for the Treasury=E2=80=99s Office of Foreign Asse=
ts Control, who discussed details on condition of anonymity.=E2=80=9CClearl=
y this latest announcement by the U.S. Treasury is designed to put addition=
al pressure on the Russians,=E2=80=9D said Gary Kirk, a portfolio manager a=
t TwentyFour Asset Management. =E2=80=9CThe alternative payment methods are=
 significantly more punitive and more challenging for Russia and hence it d=
oes increase the chances of a technical default.=E2=80=9DElsewhere, here=E2=
=80=99s a story about a  Russian negative basis trade:The pitch from Wall S=
treet sales desks to clients goes something like this: Block out the horror=
s of the war in Ukraine, and focus on the trading opportunity it has create=
d. Years, even decades could go by, one hedge-fund firm recalls being told,=
 before another relative-value trade this attractive comes along.For those =
comfortable with step one, the bet has indeed racked up quick gains. Known =
as a negative-basis trade, at its core it takes advantage of the near-unpre=
cedented selloff in Russian debt since Vladimir Putin=E2=80=99s widely cond=
emned invasion. It involves buying beaten down government or corporate bond=
s along with corresponding credit-default swaps, which insure the debtholde=
r in the event of non-payment.In normal times, the economics of such a tran=
saction don=E2=80=99t make much sense. The cost of the debt and the hedges =
move inversely, typically offsetting each other. But for much of the past m=
onth, as institutional investors were rushing to offload their stakes in Ru=
ssian assets amid mounting public outrage, bond prices were falling faster =
than the cost to hedge was rising.What is this trade? Like, you are getting=
 paid a lot of money to take some risk. What is that risk? One very live po=
ssibility is that you are taking the risk of CDS not working. Sometimes a b=
ond defaults and you lose a lot of money on the bond, and you own CDS and e=
xpect it to pay out, but the CDS seller says =E2=80=9Csurprise!=E2=80=9D an=
d doesn=E2=80=99t pay you. CDS is a sort of insurance on bonds, and there a=
re gaps in coverage. In particular  we have discussed the possibility that =
some=C2=A0Russian government bonds might=C2=A0not be eligible for CDS deliv=
ery, and that if Russia defaults=C2=A0CDS might not pay out in a way that c=
ompensates bondholders.=C2=A0The other possibility is that you are getting =
compensated for the risk of everyone hating you for holding Russian bonds. =
Russian bonds consist of (1) a series of cash flows plus (2) a pot of moral=
 disapproval. Russian CDS consists of a series of cash flows (roughly oppos=
ite the ones on the bonds), with no particular moral disapproval (or approv=
al); Russian CDS is just a zero-sum bet between two international financial=
 institutions, referencing Russia but not actually funding it. So if you go=
 long bonds and hedge with CDS, you have hedged out the credit risk and are=
 left with just the moral disapproval. Which some investors =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
environmental, social and governance investors, high-profile public investo=
rs who answer to their clients, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0really don=E2=80=99t wa=
nt, so they have to pay someone else to take it.

We have  talked a few  times about rumors that the U.S. Securities and Exch=
ange Commission is investigating one or more big banks for doing bad stuff =
with block trades. Roughly, the idea is that some big holders of shares wou=
ld go to a bank and ask it to buy their shares, and the bank would leak new=
s about the block to its other customers, driving down the price at which i=
t bought the block. If you were one of those sellers, and the price of the =
stock went down as you were trying to sell your block, and you have been re=
ading the newspapers, you are probably thinking about suing. After all, you=
=E2=80=99re the one who lost money on the alleged block-trade bad stuff. Yo=
u might wait until the SEC brings its case, to see (1) what the SEC found o=
ut and (2) if it=E2=80=99s earmarking any money from a settlement to pay yo=
u back. Or, you know, you=E2=80=99ve read the news, you might as well sue n=
ow:An investor accused Morgan Stanley of leaking information about a large =
sale of shares of Palantir Technologies Inc., saddling it with millions of =
dollars in losses.Morgan Stanley is at the center of a broad federal invest=
igation into whether Wall Street banks told favored clients about pending s=
ales, known as block trades, that they were hired to carry out quietly. The=
 investor, Disruptive Technology Solutions LLC, and affiliated funds on Mon=
day filed a demand for arbitration against the bank with the Financial Indu=
stry Regulatory Authority, a copy of which was viewed by The Wall Street Jo=
urnal.Disruptive alleges that Morgan Stanley and a senior executive there l=
eaked information ahead of the fund=E2=80=99s sale of more than $300 millio=
n of Palantir shares in February 2021, resulting in =E2=80=9Ctens of millio=
ns of dollars in damages.=E2=80=9D Disruptive is seeking compensatory and p=
unitive damages. =E2=80=A6After the stock market closed Feb. 17, a represen=
tative of Disruptive who previously worked at Morgan Stanley texted [Morgan=
 Stanley block trading executive Pawan] Passi that =E2=80=9Cthe stock got d=
estroyed at the end,=E2=80=9D and this was =E2=80=9C[n]ot a great look,=E2=
=80=9D according to the arbitration demand. The filing says Mr. Passi =E2=
=80=9Cvociferously denied=E2=80=9D that Morgan Stanley had engaged in any f=
orm of front-running and disclaimed responsibility for the stock=E2=80=99s =
fall in a call later that night.Honestly I would not want to work in block =
trading at Morgan Stanley right now? Like if a client comes to you at noon =
looking to sell a block, in some sense you are on the hook for the price. I=
f the stock is at $40 when they come to you, and it declines to $37 by the =
close when you buy the stock, and you bid them $36.95 for the block, they a=
re going to say =E2=80=9Cthis is not a great look=E2=80=9D and sue you. Who=
 is going to believe you when you say you didn=E2=80=99t leak?=C2=A0Meanwhi=
le, if it goes up to $42 by the close, you have to bid them $41.50 for the =
block; you don=E2=80=99t get the benefit of the gain. Effectively any time =
someone calls you up about a block, you have written them a put at the curr=
ent market price: If the stock goes up, the customer gets the gain, but if =
it goes down you are sort of stuck with the loss if you don=E2=80=99t want =
to get sued.

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Should Twitter have an edit button, le=

s vocally believe the answer is =E2=80=9Cyes.=E2=80=9D Here I do not want t=
o argue about the substance, though. I just want to point out that if you h=
ave a view on this question, a Twitter Inc. board member is currently <a hr=

; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: solid; background-color: #ccc;">conducting a poll about it</a>, on Twit=
ter. (=E2=80=9CYes=E2=80=9D is outrunning =E2=80=9Cno=E2=80=9D by about 3 t=
o 1.) =E2=80=9CThe consequences of this poll will be important,=E2=80=9D <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0=

le: solid; background-color: #ccc;">tweeted</a> Twitter=E2=80=99s chief exe=
cutive officer. So go vote, and whichever side wins will happen I guess.</p=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yesterday <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vc=

: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> I suggested</a> that Elon Musk, Twitter=
=E2=80=99s new largest shareholder, should join the board of directors and =
live-tweet the board meetings. Today <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0Fy=

r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Twitter announced</a> that =
Musk is in fact joining the board, and I feel like it=E2=80=99s going to be=
 so much worse than live-tweeting the board meetings? It=E2=80=99s going to=
 be:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: =
16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Musk thinks of a way to make =
Twitter more annoying (the edit button, more =E2=80=9Cfree speech,=E2=80=9D=
 somehow more Dogecoin integration, etc.).</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-botto=
m: 5px;">Musk conducts a Twitter poll asking if Twitter should do the thing=
.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CYes, be more annoying=
=E2=80=9D wins in a landslide; how could it not.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">Musk shows up at the board meeting to be like =E2=80=9Csee, =
our users want this annoying thing.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bot=
tom: 5px;">Also he owns the most shares.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom:=
 5px;">The board does the annoying thing.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom=
: 5px;">Emboldened, Musk returns to Step 1.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">I also joked yesterday that Musk would be the first Twitter user eve=
r to join the board. That is not literally true, but it does=C2=A0<em>feel=
=C2=A0</em>true.=C2=A0My basic model of how Twitter works is that everyone =
who works at Twitter hates the product and its users, which <a href=3D"http=
s://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0d=
GVyLmNvbS9tYXR0X2xldmluZS9zdGF0dXMvMTQ2NTMyNzg4NjM0OTYxNTEwNg/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cBa6df3360" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">creates=C2=A0a healthy tension</a>. Twitter=
=E2=80=99s users push Twitter to be more Twitter-like, to appeal to the wei=
rdest and most addicted tendencies of its most online power users. But Twit=
ter=E2=80=99s employees and executives push it to be less Twitter-like, bec=
ause they don=E2=80=99t use it, and it ends up being just about Twitter-lik=
e enough. If you put Twitter=E2=80=99s most addicted users in charge, it wo=
uld be an unusable nightmare of harassment and weirdness. Twitter=E2=80=99s=
 very most addicted user is now in charge.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
What else, what else. Is Musk =E2=80=9Cin charge=E2=80=9D of Twitter? He is=
 just one of 12 board members.[2] He=E2=80=99s the largest shareholder, but=
 with 9.2%=C2=A0 of the stock he=E2=80=99s not really a controlling shareho=
lder. As part of his deal to join the board, he <a href=3D"https://link.mai=
l.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9Bc=
mNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE0MTgwOTEvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTIyMDk1NjUxL2QzNDIyNTdkZXgx=
MDEuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB49d863f7" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscop=
e=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration:=
 none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; b=
order-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">signed a standstill agr=
eement</a>=C2=A0promising not to acquire more than 14.9% of the stock, so h=
e can=E2=80=99t threaten a hostile takeover if he doesn=E2=80=99t get his w=
ay on the board.=C2=A0The company is run by a CEO and executives who were n=
ot appointed by Musk and do not report directly to him.</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">On the other hand, Musk is surely Twitter=E2=80=99s noisiest b=
oard member. He <em>is=C2=A0</em>the biggest shareholder. Since Donald Trum=
p was kicked off Twitter, Musk is arguably its most important=C2=A0<em>user=
</em>. He has tweeted memes comparing Twitter=E2=80=99s CEO, Parag Agrawal,=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDQtMDQvbXVzay1z=
LXR3aXR0ZXItbW92ZS1mb2xsb3dzLWhpbnQtYXQtdW1icmFnZS13aXRoLWRvcnNleS1zLWV4aXQ=
_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQwNTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW=
5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwNDA1JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cB7dda9541" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" t=
arget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> to Stalin</a>. (=E2=80=9CThis could be =
setting up for another leadership change,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZX=
JnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDQtMDQvd29vZC1zZWVzLXBvdGVudGlhbC1mb3Itd=

" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;"> Cathie Wood said</a> of Musk=E2=80=99s stake.) And he=E2=80=99s =
gonna show up at board meetings with those polls. It is hard not to conclud=
e that he will have an outsized influence on the board, that he will not be=
 especially deferential to Agrawal=E2=80=99s vision,=C2=A0and that Twitter =
might shift its strategy in some way =E2=80=94=C2=A0possibly toward less mo=
deration of misinformation and abuse =E2=80=94=C2=A0because of his presence=
.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CLooking forward to working with Parag & Twitter board t=
o make significant improvements to Twitter in coming months,=E2=80=9D <a hr=

le: solid; background-color: #ccc;">he tweeted today</a>.</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">As we discussed yesterday, Musk disclosed his 9.2% stake in =
Twitter on Schedule 13G, which is traditionally used by passive investors. =
You are not allowed to file a 13G =E2=80=94=C2=A0you have to file the more =
detailed and informative Schedule 13D =E2=80=94=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://lin=

1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">if you have</a> =E2=80=9Cacquired the securities with any purp=
ose, or with the effect, of changing or influencing the control of the issu=
er.=E2=80=9D Did Musk acquire his Twitter stock with the purpose of influen=
cing the control of Twitter?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99=
t know? I think it is quite obvious that if you acquire 9.2% of the stock, =
file a 13G, and immediately launch a proxy fight to replace the CEO and boa=
rd of directors, you have broken the rules. It is fairly obvious that if yo=
u acquire 9.2% of the stock, file a 13G, and go to the company saying =E2=
=80=9CI own 9.2% and will launch a proxy fight unless you appoint me to you=
r board of directors,=E2=80=9D you have also broken the rules. If you acqui=
re 9.2% of the stock, file a 13G, tweet some ominous polls, cook up various=
 schemes to tweak the product, have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9wYXJhZ2Evc3Rhd=
HVzLzE1MTEzMjA5NTM1OTgzNTc1MDU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBebcd2a1b" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
some basically polite=C2=A0chats with the CEO</a> and get appointed to the =
board, have you broken the rules? I think that you have not clearly broken =
the letter of the rules.[3] Nonetheless I think that the SEC=E2=80=99s Elon=
 Musk Division is going to be annoyed. Passive investors aren=E2=80=99t usu=
ally asked to sign standstills promising not to buy more stock.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is also a=C2=A0<em>timing=C2=A0</em>point that t=
hey might find annoying. I don=E2=80=99t know when Elon Musk bought his 9.2=
% stake in Twitter, or how, or even what he actually bought. (Maybe it=E2=

ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> do not quite understand</a> the =
geopolitical calculus around Russia paying or not paying its debts? Like:</=
p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0=
;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Russia has dollars that it holds in=
 U.S. bank accounts, which have been more or less immobilized by sanctions.=
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It seems like the U.S. would like=
 it if Russia used those dollars to pay its foreign debts, since (1) the do=
llars would go to foreign investors (U.S. pension funds, etc.), (2) Russia =
would have fewer dollars=C2=A0and (3) Russia wouldn=E2=80=99t get anything =
in exchange.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It seems like Russia =
might not want to use those dollars to pay its foreign debts, since (1) the=
n it would have fewer dollars, (2) it wouldn=E2=80=99t get anything in exch=
ange and (3) the dollars would go to creditors from =E2=80=9Cunfriendly=E2=
=80=9D countries.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"><em>In general</=
em>=C2=A0Russia would prefer not to default on its debts, in order to maint=
ain the confidence of international capital markets, but it=E2=80=99s not l=
ike Russia has much access to international capital markets anyway? The=C2=
=A0<em>sanctions=C2=A0</em>are the main problem for Russia, as well as a ge=
neral capital boycott due to its brutal invasion of Ukraine, and paying bon=
d interest on time won=E2=80=99t fix any of that.</li></ol><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">But in fact Russia has worked hard to pay its dollar bondholde=
rs=C2=A0and has been successful so far, and now the U.S. is <a href=3D"http=
s://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27264822.267602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY=

dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;"> trying harder to stop it</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Russia=E2=80=99s efforts to avoid a sovereign default too=
k another blow after the U.S. Treasury halted dollar debt payments from the=
 country=E2=80=99s accounts at U.S. banks.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
The decision further complicates Russia=E2=80=99s attempts to keep meeting =
debt obligations amid the sanctions imposed after it invaded Ukraine. As th=
e government tries to sidestep its first external default in about a centur=

ranged from positive:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The [firs=
t] mix flows well with lots of energy and has a nice groove. Transitions an=
d timings are pretty spot on so overall strong mix for this type of genre. =

te><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Through meh:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s cheerfully competent and inoffensive, VIP room-s=
tyle vocal house and disco. A little obvious at times, but he seems to be h=
aving fun.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To negative:<=
/p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To my mind, being brutal about =
it, this cheeseball needs to do a few more bar gigs to learn how to build a=
 set better and gain a deeper knowledge of dance music =E2=80=94 not just p=
lump for obvious, commercial, accessible tracks. The person is obviously ha=
ving fun, and I wouldn=E2=80=99t want to put anyone off. But without demons=
trating a knowledge of more quality and depth in their house music selectio=
ns, they aren=E2=80=99t going to progress very far in their DJ career and s=
hould probably not give up the day job quite yet.</p></blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah, well, the day job pays pretty well. Wouldn=E2=80=
=99t it be amazing if Solomon picked up DJing as a middle-age hobby and=C2=
=A0<em>did=C2=A0</em>become the most creative and influential dance music D=
J in the world? Disclosure, I used to work at Goldman, and I feel like ther=
e was a time when that was the expectation. Sure, whatever, have a hobby, j=
